required.title: [Primary Texts] Major works from the Standing Ground literary canon
[Reference] The Craft of Research
required.author: Wayne C. Booth, Gregory G. Colomb, and Joseph M. Williams

suggested.title: [Reference] Literary Theory: A Very Short Introduction
suggested.author: Jonathan Culler

suggested.title: [Reference] Critical Terms for Literary Study
suggested.author: edited by Frank Lentricchia and Thomas McLaughlin

suggested.title: [For Mentor] How to Read Literature Like a Professor
suggested.author: Thomas C. Foster

competence_markers.description: The student can formulate an original research question about a literary text that addresses a genuine gap in existing scholarship
The student can conduct a literature review of at least eight scholarly sources, mapping the existing conversation and identifying where their research fits
The student understands and can apply multiple analytical methods (close reading, historical-contextual, comparative, thematic, formal) and can explain why they chose their approach
The student can design a research project with clear scope, appropriate methods, and a realistic timeline
The student can build an annotated bibliography with proper citation, source summaries, and evaluative commentary
The student can position their argument within existing scholarship — explaining what they are building on, responding to, or filling in
The student can articulate the limitations of their research honestly
The student has produced a complete research proposal that demonstrates readiness for the extended thesis in C2

narration_prompts.prompt: What is your research question, and how did you arrive at it?
narration_prompts.detail: What did you think you wanted to study when you started, and how did the literature review change your focus?

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through your literature review.
narration_prompts.detail: Who are the key scholars? Where do they agree? Where do they disagree? Where is the gap your research addresses?
Why did you choose this approach? What does it reveal that other approaches might miss? What are its limitations?

narration_prompts.prompt: How does your argument relate to existing scholarship?
narration_prompts.detail: Are you extending someone's work? Challenging it? Filling a gap? How would you position your argument in a paragraph?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the strongest objection to your thesis?
narration_prompts.detail: How would you address it? Is there evidence that could undermine your reading?
How did you manage your time? What tools did you use? What would you do differently next time?

cross_connections.description: Advanced research methodology and the ethics of scholarship are deeply intertwined. The literature review, source evaluation, and scholarly positioning taught here are practiced at the highest level in 3.2.6-C. The two packets should be taken concurrently.
C1 teaches the methodology; C2 teaches the execution. The research design and literature review produced here become the foundation of the extended thesis written in C2.

Historical research methodology parallels literary research methodology. The student who can conduct a literature review and position an argument in literary scholarship can do the same in historical scholarship.

While literary research is qualitative and scientific research is often quantitative, both require hypothesis formation, evidence gathering, honest acknowledgment of limitations, and positioning within existing knowledge. The student benefits from understanding both traditions.
Philosophical scholarship uses the same tools — literature review, argument construction, engagement with existing thinkers. The methodological skills transfer directly.
Understanding qualitative versus quantitative reasoning helps the student see literary research as a rigorous discipline with its own standards, not a lesser form of scientific inquiry.

content.why_this_matters:
You know how to find sources, take notes, cite properly, and write a thesis-driven essay. Now you are going to learn how to design and execute original research — the kind of work that produces genuine new knowledge about a literary text or tradition. This is not a longer version of the Weathering Storms research paper. It is a qualitatively different activity: formulating a research question that has not been definitively answered, conducting a literature review to understand what has already been said, choosing and applying appropriate analytical methods, and constructing an argument that contributes something new to the scholarly conversation.

Original research is the highest form of intellectual work. It requires everything you have built so far — reading discipline, writing precision, source evaluation, citation integrity — and adds to it the capacity to see something that other careful readers have not seen, or to see something familiar from a genuinely new angle. Not every piece of original research will be groundbreaking. But every piece will require you to move beyond summarizing what others have said and toward saying something of your own — grounded in evidence, informed by scholarship, and honest about its limitations.

This packet teaches the methodology. The next packet (C2) teaches the execution — producing a publication-quality extended thesis and defending it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Original research design** — the student can formulate a research question that has not been definitively answered and design a plan to investigate it
2. **Literature review** — the student can survey existing scholarship on a topic, identify what has been said, and locate gaps or tensions in the existing conversation
3. **Analytical methods** — the student understands and can apply multiple approaches to literary analysis: close reading, historical-contextual, comparative, thematic, and formal
4. **Qualitative reasoning** — the student can construct interpretive arguments that are rigorous, evidence-based, and intellectually honest about their limitations
5. **Research planning** — the student can create a research timeline, manage sources, and sustain a multi-week investigation
6. **Scholarly conversation** — the student can position their own argument within the context of existing scholarship, engaging with other critics as conversation partners
content.core_content:
### Part 1: From Research Paper to Original Research

In Weathering Storms, you wrote research papers: you found a question, gathered sources, and constructed an argument. That was important work, and the skills it built are the foundation for what comes next. But there is a difference between a research paper and original research.

A research paper synthesizes existing knowledge. You read what scholars have said and organize it into a coherent argument. Original research goes further: you identify something that existing scholarship has not adequately addressed — a gap, a tension, an overlooked pattern — and you investigate it yourself. Your argument does not merely report what others have found. It adds something to the conversation.

This does not mean you need to overturn centuries of literary criticism. Original research can be modest: a close reading of a specific passage that reveals a pattern no one has explicitly analyzed, a comparison between two texts that illuminates both, a historical-contextual investigation that changes how a scene should be understood. The originality is in the thinking, not the scale.

### Part 2: The Literature Review

Before you can say something new, you need to know what has already been said. The literature review is a systematic survey of existing scholarship on your topic. It serves three purposes:

1. **It prevents you from reinventing the wheel.** If someone has already made your argument, you need to know — so you can either build on it, refine it, or redirect your research.

2. **It reveals the conversation.** Scholarship is not a collection of isolated arguments. It is a conversation — scholars respond to each other, disagree, refine, and extend. Your literature review shows you where the conversation has been and where it might go next.

3. **It identifies gaps.** The most productive research questions often emerge from reading existing scholarship and noticing: What has not been said? Where do scholars disagree without resolution? What evidence has been overlooked?

**How to conduct a literature review:**

- Begin with the most recent comprehensive study of your text or topic. Read its bibliography to identify the key scholars and works.
- Work backward through the scholarship, reading the most important contributions. You do not need to read everything — you need to read the works that have shaped the conversation.
- As you read, take notes on: What does each scholar argue? What evidence do they use? Where do they agree with other scholars? Where do they disagree? What questions do they leave unanswered?
- Organize your review thematically, not chronologically. Group scholars by the positions they take, not by when they published.
- Write a literature review of 500-1,000 words that maps the existing conversation and identifies the gap your research will address.

### Part 3: Research Design

Once you have a question and a sense of what has already been said, you need a plan. Research design in literary study is less formal than in the sciences, but it still requires deliberate choices about method and scope.

**Key decisions in research design:**

**What is your primary text (or texts)?** Be specific. "Shakespeare" is not a manageable scope. "The role of the Fool in *King Lear*" is.

**What is your analytical method?**
- **Close reading:** Detailed analysis of specific passages, attending to language, imagery, structure, and form. The foundation of all literary analysis.
- **Historical-contextual:** Reading the text in light of the historical circumstances of its composition. What was happening when this was written, and how does that context change our understanding?
- **Comparative:** Reading two or more texts together to illuminate both. What does reading *The Iliad* alongside *The Things They Carried* reveal about how war narratives work?
- **Thematic:** Tracing a theme (justice, redemption, power, identity) across a text or set of texts.
- **Formal/structural:** Analyzing how the text's form — its structure, its genre conventions, its narrative technique — creates its meaning.

Most strong literary research combines methods. A close reading grounded in historical context, or a comparative study driven by thematic analysis. Choose the methods that best serve your question.

**What is your scope?** Define what you will and will not address. A research project that tries to say everything about *Hamlet* will say nothing well. A project that focuses on Hamlet's relationship to performance and theatricality within the play can say something specific and substantive.

**What are your sources?** Plan your source list: primary texts, secondary scholarship, and any contextual materials you will need. Estimate how many sources you need (for a Standing Ground project, typically 8-15 scholarly sources in addition to primary texts).

### Part 4: Qualitative Reasoning in Literary Research

Literary research is qualitative — it works with interpretation, not data. This does not make it less rigorous. It means the rigor takes a different form.

**What makes a qualitative literary argument rigorous:**

- **Evidence is specific and textual.** Every claim is grounded in specific words, passages, or patterns from the primary text. General impressions are not evidence.
- **Interpretation is argued, not asserted.** You do not just declare what a passage means — you show why your interpretation is more convincing than alternatives.
- **Alternative readings are acknowledged.** A rigorous argument addresses the strongest objection or alternative interpretation, not just the weakest.
- **Limitations are stated honestly.** "This reading is limited to the first three acts" or "This analysis does not account for the biographical dimension" — honesty about scope strengthens rather than weakens your argument.
- **The argument is falsifiable in principle.** There should be evidence that could, in principle, undermine your reading. If nothing could possibly count against your interpretation, it is not an argument — it is a statement of faith.

### Part 5: Managing a Sustained Research Project

Original research takes weeks or months, not days. Managing this process requires planning.

**A research timeline:**
- **Week 1-2:** Question formulation. Read the primary text closely. Develop initial questions.
- **Week 3-4:** Literature review. Find and read existing scholarship. Identify the gap.
- **Week 5:** Research design. Choose methods, define scope, plan source list.
- **Week 6-8:** Deep analysis. Close reading of primary text with research question in focus. Continued engagement with secondary sources. Extensive note-taking.
- **Week 9-10:** Drafting. Write the first draft of the research paper or thesis.
- **Week 11-12:** Revision. Structural revision, then paragraph-level, then sentence-level. Verify all citations.

This is a sample timeline. Your actual project may be shorter or longer. The point is that you plan the process, not just the product.

**Research management tools:**
- A dedicated research notebook or digital file for every project
- An annotated bibliography that grows as you find sources (recording not just citation information but a brief summary of each source's argument and your response to it)
- Regular check-ins with your mentor to discuss progress, problems, and evolving ideas

### Part 6: Positioning Your Argument in the Scholarly Conversation

Your research does not exist in a vacuum. It enters a conversation that has been going on, in many cases, for centuries. Part of advanced research is learning to position your argument: explaining to the reader where it fits, what it responds to, and what it contributes.

**Moves for positioning your argument:**

- **"While scholars have focused on X, this paper examines Y."** Shows you know the existing conversation and are going somewhere new.
- **"Building on [Scholar]'s argument that..., I argue further that..."** Shows you are extending existing work.
- **"[Scholar A] argues X; [Scholar B] argues Y. I contend that both miss Z."** Shows you are resolving a tension in existing scholarship.
- **"No existing study has examined [specific aspect]. This paper addresses that gap."** Shows you have identified something genuinely unexplored.

These are not formulas. They are ways of thinking about how your work relates to other people's work. The student who can position their argument is demonstrating scholarly maturity — the understanding that knowledge is built collectively, and that your contribution is most valuable when you show how it connects to what came before.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Literature Review Exercise [Early in Packet]
Choose a literary text or topic. Find at least eight scholarly sources. Write a 500-word literature review that maps the existing conversation: who are the key scholars, what do they argue, where do they agree and disagree, and what gaps exist? Discuss with your mentor: what question emerges from the gaps you identified?

### Study 2: Method Comparison [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Take a single passage from a primary text (20-30 lines). Analyze it using three different methods: close reading, historical-contextual, and thematic. Write a paragraph for each. Discuss: How does the method change what you see? Which method reveals the most about this passage? Why?

### Study 3: Research Design Document [Mid-Packet]
Write a one-page research design document for your project: research question, primary text(s), analytical method(s), scope and limitations, planned sources, and timeline. Present it to your mentor for feedback. Revise based on discussion.

### Study 4: Annotated Bibliography [Mid-Packet]
Build an annotated bibliography of at least ten sources for your research project. Each entry includes: proper citation, a 3-4 sentence summary of the source's argument, and a 2-3 sentence evaluation of how the source relates to your own project. This bibliography becomes the foundation of your literature review.

### Study 5: Peer Review of Research Design [Late Packet]
Exchange research design documents with another student (or present to a small group). Provide specific feedback: Is the question clear and answerable? Is the scope manageable? Are the methods appropriate? What potential problems do you see? Revise based on feedback.

### Study 6: Draft Literature Review and Research Proposal [End of Packet]
Write a complete research proposal (1,500-2,000 words) that includes: your research question, a literature review surveying existing scholarship, your proposed method and scope, and a preliminary thesis. This proposal is the launching pad for the extended thesis in C2.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Research Question Quality
**Developing well:** The question is specific, genuinely investigative, and addresses a gap in existing scholarship. The student can explain why the question matters and how it connects to the scholarly conversation.

**Developing slowly:** The question is too broad, too obvious, or duplicates existing scholarship without adding anything new.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student done enough preliminary reading to know what has already been said? Have you helped them narrow their focus? Can they articulate what would count as a good answer?

### 2. Literature Review Competence
**Developing well:** The review maps the existing conversation accurately, identifies key positions, and locates a genuine gap. The student represents other scholars' arguments fairly and can explain where their own work fits.

**Developing slowly:** The review is a list of summaries without synthesis. The student cannot explain the conversation — only individual sources.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student been taught to organize thematically rather than chronologically? Can they identify two scholars who disagree and explain the nature of the disagreement?

### 3. Methodological Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can name their analytical method, explain why they chose it, and articulate what it reveals and what it misses. They understand that method is a choice, not a default.

**Developing slowly:** The student does not think about method — they just "read and write about" the text without deliberate analytical framework.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student practiced the method comparison exercise? Can they analyze the same passage from multiple angles and see the difference?

### 4. Research Management
**Developing well:** The student plans their work, maintains organized notes and an annotated bibliography, checks in regularly, and adjusts the timeline when necessary. The project stays on track.

**Developing slowly:** The student procrastinates, loses notes, and produces the project in a rush at the end. The quality suffers from poor planning.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student created a timeline? Are you checking in at regular intervals? Has the student been given enough structure in the early stages to build self-management habits?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Letting the Student Skip the Literature Review
Students often want to jump straight to their argument. Do not allow this. The literature review serves a critical function — it prevents the student from making an argument that has already been made (or refuted) by someone else. It also deepens the student's understanding of the text. Insist on a thorough review before the student begins their own analysis.

### Allowing an Unmanageable Scope
The most common design flaw in student research is attempting too much. "The theme of justice in Shakespeare" is a dissertation, not a Standing Ground project. Help the student define a scope that is achievable within their timeframe and page length. Smaller scope with deeper analysis is always better than broader scope with superficial treatment.

### Confusing Difficulty with Rigor
Some students (and mentors) assume that the most rigorous approach is the most theoretically complex. It is not. A careful close reading of a specific passage, grounded in the text and informed by historical context, can be more rigorous than a theoretically elaborate argument that loses touch with the actual words on the page. Rigor means thoroughness, precision, and honesty — not obscurity.

### Neglecting the Primary Text
It is possible to become so absorbed in secondary scholarship that the primary text fades into the background. The student's argument must be grounded in the literary text itself, not merely in what other scholars have said about it. If the student's paper could have been written without reading the primary text, something has gone wrong.
